branchingScenario.title: AN48.1-8 | Pelvic wall and viscera — Clinical Case

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 65-year-old multiparous woman presents with a sensation of something coming down per vaginum. Work through this case exploring pelvic floor anatomy, visceral relationships, and clinical procedures.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified pelvic floor anatomy, uterine prolapse mechanisms, and suprapubic cystostomy landmarks.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review the pelvic diaphragm, puborectalis function, and BPH anatomy.
Revisit levator ani, puborectalis, internal iliac artery, pelvic types, and surgical landmarks for cystostomy.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 65-year-old woman, para 5, presents with a mass descending through the vaginal introitus, worse on straining. She also reports urinary stress incontinence.

This is uterine prolapse, resulting from weakening of the levator ani muscle and the cardinal and uterosacral ligaments. The pelvic diaphragm is formed primarily by the levator ani and the coccygeus muscle.

The puborectalis sling (part of levator ani) maintains the anorectal angle of approximately 90 degrees, essential for faecal continence. The nerve supply to levator ani comes from S3 and S4 nerve roots.

branchingQuestion.question: What is the primary muscle forming the pelvic diaphragm, and what structure maintains the anorectal angle for faecal continence?

alternatives.text: Levator ani forms the pelvic diaphragm; the puborectalis sling maintains the anorectal angle
The obturator internus forms the pelvic diaphragm; the external sphincter maintains the angle

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Obturator internus forms the lateral pelvic wall, not the diaphragm. The pelvic diaphragm is primarily levator ani, and the puborectalis sling maintains the anorectal angle.

alternatives.text: The piriformis forms the pelvic diaphragm; the coccygeus maintains the angle

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Piriformis is a posterior pelvic wall muscle. The pelvic diaphragm is primarily levator ani, and the puborectalis sling maintains the ~90-degree anorectal angle.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Levator ani is the primary pelvic diaphragm muscle, and puborectalis maintains the anorectal angle.

The patient's husband, aged 70, accompanies her. He has been experiencing urinary hesitancy and poor stream. Examination reveals an enlarged prostate. In benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH), the median (middle) lobe of the prostate enlarges and obstructs the internal urethral orifice.

The internal iliac artery is the main arterial supply to the pelvic viscera, pelvic walls, and perineum. The pelvic inlet is bounded posteriorly by the promontory of the sacrum.

The female pelvis is classified as gynaecoid type — rounded inlet, subpubic angle >90 degrees.

branchingQuestion.question: The husband develops acute urinary retention from BPH. A suprapubic cystostomy is planned. Where should the trocar be inserted?

alternatives.text: Two centimetres above the pubic symphysis when the bladder is distended, avoiding the peritoneum
At the umbilicus in the midline for best access to the distended bladder

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The umbilicus is too high and would risk entering the peritoneal cavity. Suprapubic cystostomy is performed 2 cm above the pubic symphysis with a distended bladder.

alternatives.text: Through the perineum, posterior to the scrotum

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: That approach is used for perineal urethrostomy, not cystostomy. Suprapubic cystostomy is performed 2 cm above the pubic symphysis when the bladder is distended.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key pelvic anatomy:

  • Pelvic diaphragm: Levator ani + coccygeus; nerve supply S3-S4
  • Puborectalis: Maintains anorectal angle (~90°) for continence
  • Uterine prolapse: Weakened levator ani + cardinal/uterosacral ligaments
  • BPH: Median lobe obstructs internal urethral orifice
  • Internal iliac artery: Main pelvic blood supply
  • Suprapubic cystostomy: 2 cm above pubic symphysis with distended bladder
  • Gynaecoid pelvis: Rounded inlet, subpubic angle >90°
